name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ ٱلْحَمْدُ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١)
```

```english
Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah. To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise, and He has power over all things. (1)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ فَمِنكُمْ كَافِرٌ وَمِنكُم مُّؤْمِنٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٢)
```

```english
It is He who created you. Then some of you are faithless and some of you are faithful, and Allah watches what you do. (2)
```

```arabic
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٣)
```

```english
He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom, and He formed you and perfected your forms, and toward Him is your destination. (3)
```

```arabic
يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُسِرُّونَ وَمَا تُعْلِنُونَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٤)
```

```english
He knows whatever there is in the heavens and the earth, and He knows whatever you hide and whatever you disclose, and Allah knows best what is in your breasts. (4)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ فَذَاقُوا۟ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٥)
```

```english
Has there not come to you the account of those who were faithless before? They tasted the evil consequence of their conduct, and there is a painful punishment for them. (5)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُۥ كَانَت تَّأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَقَالُوٓا۟ أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنَا فَكَفَرُوا۟ وَتَوَلَّوا۟ ۚ وَّٱسْتَغْنَى ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (٦)
```

```english
That was because their apostles would bring them manifest proofs, but they said, ‘Will humans be our guides?!’ So they disbelieved and turned away, and Allah had no need [of their faith] and Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable. (6)
```

```arabic
زَعَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن لَّن يُبْعَثُوا۟ ۚ قُلْ بَلَىٰ وَرَبِّى لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِمَا عَمِلْتُمْ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (٧)
```

```english
The faithless claim that they will not be resurrected. Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will surely be informed of what you did, and that is easy for Allah.’ (7)
```

```arabic
فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلنُّورِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلْنَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٨)
```

```english
So have faith in Allah and His Apostle and the light, which We have sent down, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (8)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُكُمْ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْجَمْعِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمُ ٱلتَّغَابُنِ ۗ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صَـٰلِحًا يُكَفِّرْ عَنْهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِۦ وَيُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٩)
```

```english
When He will you bring you together for the Day of Gathering, it will be a day of privation [and regret]. As for those who have faith in Allah and act righteously, He shall absolve them of their misdeeds and admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. That is the great success. (9)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٠)
```

```english
But as for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they will be the inmates of the Fire, to remain in it [forever], and it is an evil destination. (10)
```

## (Commentary)

This *surah* (سورة) — *a chapter of the Qur’an* — is similar to *Surah al-Hadid* in its context (سياق) and its composition (نظم), as if it were a summary of it. Its purpose is to urge the believers and incite their desire to spend in the way of Allah, and to remove what haunts their hearts and creeps into their souls of grief and regret over the calamities that strike them while enduring the hardships of faith in Allah, striving in His way, and spending therein, by clarifying that all of that occurs by the permission of Allah.

The verses we have cited from the beginning of the *surah* serve as an introduction and a preamble to clarify the aforementioned purpose. They demonstrate that His Beautiful Names (أسماؤه الحسنى) and His Sublime Attributes (صفاته العليا) necessitate the Resurrection (البعث) and the return of all to Allah—a return in which the people of faith and righteous deeds (الصالحات) are led to an eternal Paradise, and the people of disbelief and denial to an everlasting Fire. Thus, it is a preparation for the command to obey Allah and His Messenger, to be patient in the face of calamities, and to spend in the way of Allah without being affected by the prevention of any hinderer or the fear of any blamer's reproach.

The *surah* is Medinan (مدنية), as evidenced by the context of its verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يُسَبِّحُ لِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ» **Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth glorifies Allah. To Him belongs all sovereignty and to Him belongs all praise, and He has power over all things.** (64:1). The discussion regarding the meaning of glorification (التسبيح), sovereignty (الملك), praise (الحمد), and power (القدرة) has preceded, as has the point that "whatever is in the heavens and the earth" includes the heavens and the earth themselves, as well as whoever and whatever is within them.

His saying: «لَهُ الْمُلْكُ» (**To Him belongs all sovereignty**) is absolute (مطلق), implying the unrestricted nature of His sovereignty, unconfined by any limit and unconditioned by any stipulation or proviso. Thus, no decree is effective except His decree, and no decree of His exists except that it is effective over whatever He wills.

Similarly, His saying: «وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ» (**and to Him belongs all praise**) is absolute, implying that every praise from every praiser returns to Him—and praise (الحمد) is the commendation of a voluntary beauty (الثناء على الجميل الاختياري)—because creation (الخلق) and command (الأمر) belong to Him. Therefore, there is no essence (ذات), attribute (صفة), or action (فعل) that is beautiful and praiseworthy except that it is from Him and returns to Him.

Likewise, His saying: «وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ» (**and He has power over all things**) indicates the universality of the object of His power, which is neither limited nor restricted by any condition or stipulation.

Since the verses—as previously indicated—are directed toward proving the Return (المعاد) — *the restoration of life after death for judgment* — this verse serves as the first premise for its proof. It conveys that Allah is transcendent (منزه) above every deficiency and blemish in His essence, attributes, and acts; He possesses the authority to judge over everything and to dispose of it however He wishes and wills—and He only disposes in a beautiful manner. His power encompasses everything; thus, He has the right to dispose of His creation by restoration (الإعادة) just as He disposed of them by origination (الإبداء)—both creation (الإحداث) and preservation (الإبقاء). He has the right to resurrect them if His will attaches to it, and it only attaches according to His wisdom (حكمه).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ فَمِنْكُمْ كافِرٌ وَمِنْكُمْ مُؤْمِنٌ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ» **It is He who created you: yet some of you are faithless and some of you are faithful, and Allah watches what you do.** (64:2). The letter *fa* (ف) in «فَمِنْكُمْ» (**yet some of you**) indicates the mere sequential occurrence of disbelief (الكفر) and faith (الإيمان) following creation. Thus, there is no indication in this derivation (التفريع) as to whether disbelief and faith are created by Allah or not. Rather, the meaning is their branching into two groups: some of them disbelievers and some of them believers. The mention of the disbeliever was placed first due to the abundance and prevalence of disbelievers.

The word "from" (من) in His saying «فَمِنْكُمْ» and «فَمِنْكُمْ» is for partitivity (التبعيض); meaning, some of you are disbelievers and some of you are believers.

He has cautioned by His saying: «وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ» (**and Allah watches what you do**) that their division into two parts and their separation into two groups is a reality as mentioned, and they are distinct before Him because the criterion in that is their deeds (أعمالهم), both outward and inward. Allah is seeing of what they do; nothing is hidden from Him, nor is anything confused.

The verse contains another premise for proving the Return and its necessity: that people are created by Allah, the Exalted, and are distinguished before Him by disbelief and faith, and by righteous and wicked deeds.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ» **He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom, and He formed you and perfected your forms, and toward Him is the return.** (64:3). What is meant by "truth" (الحق) is the opposite of "falsehood" (الباطل), which is creating them without a fixed end (غاية) or a fixed purpose (غرض), as He said: «لَوْ أَرَدْنا أَنْ نَتَّخِذَ لَهْواً لَاتَّخَذْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا» **Had We desired to take up a diversion, We would have taken it up from those near Us** (21:17), and He said: «وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ» **We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with consummate wisdom, but most of them do not know.** (44:38-39).

His saying: «وَصَوَّرَكُمْ فَأَحْسَنَ صُوَرَكُمْ» (**and He formed you and perfected your forms**): what is meant by "forming" (التصوير) is the granting of a form (الصورة), and the form of a thing is its constitution (قوامه) and the mode of its existence (نحو وجوده), as He said: «لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ» **We certainly created man in the best of forms** (95:4). The beauty of the form (حسن الصورة) is the harmony of its components with one another and the harmony of the whole with the purpose of its existence. It is not "beauty" in the sense of a handsome appearance or prettiness, but rather the general beauty (الحسن العام) that permeates all things, as the Exalted said: «الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ» **[He] who perfected everything He created** (32:7).

Perhaps the specific mention of the beauty of their forms is to draw attention to the fact that they are suited for the ultimate end, which is the return to Allah. Thus, the sentence would be among the premises directed toward proving the Return, as previously indicated.

With this verse, the premises resulting in the necessity of the Resurrection and the return of creation to Allah, the Exalted, are completed. For since He, the Exalted, is the absolute Sovereign and Powerful, He has the right to judge as He wills and dispose as He desires; He is transcendent above every deficiency and blemish, and is praiseworthy in His acts. Since people differ in disbelief and faith, and He is seeing of their deeds, and since creation was for a purpose without futility or randomness, it was necessary that they be resurrected after their worldly life for another life—permanent and eternal—wherein they live a lasting life according to what their difference in disbelief and faith necessitates. This is the recompense (الجزاء) by which their believers find felicity and their disbelievers find wretchedness.

To this conclusion He points by His saying: «وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ» (**and toward Him is the return**).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما تُسِرُّونَ وَما تُعْلِنُونَ وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ» **He knows whatever there is in the heavens and the earth, and He knows what you keep secret and what you disclose, and Allah knows best what is in the breasts.** (64:4). This is a refutation of a doubt held by those who deny the Return, based on the perceived improbability (الاستبعاد) of it: how can existing things be restored when they have perished and vanished, while the events of the world are beyond counting and the deeds and attributes are innumerable—some being manifest and public, some hidden and secret, some witnessed, and some unseen? The answer was given that Allah knows what is in the heavens and the earth, and He knows what you keep secret and what you disclose.

His saying: «وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ» (**and Allah knows best what is in the breasts**): it is said that this is a parenthetical concluding statement (اعتراض تذييلي) confirming the encompassment of His knowledge regarding what they keep secret and what they disclose. The meaning is: He, the Exalted, encompasses with knowledge the matters (المضمرات) nestled within the breasts of people, which never depart from them; so how could anything you keep secret or disclose be hidden from Him?

In His saying: «وَاللهُ عَلِيمٌ» etc., the manifest noun (الظاهر) is placed where a pronoun (الضمير) would be—the original being "and He is knowing" (وهو عليم) etc. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to point toward the cause (علة) of the judgment, and so that it may serve as a general principle (ضابط) that functions like a proverb.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ فَذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ» **Has there not come to you the account of those who were faithless aforetime? They tasted the evil consequences of their conduct, and there is a painful punishment for them.** (64:5). The "evil consequence" (وبال) of the matter is its bad aftermath (تبعته السيئة), and what is meant by "their conduct" (أمرهم) is their disbelief and the transgressions (فسوق) that branched from it.

Since the requirement of His Beautiful Names and Sublime Attributes enumerated in the preceding verses is the necessity of the Return of people and their journey to their Lord for reckoning and recompense, it was necessary to inform them of what they must perform or avoid, which is the Law (الشرع). The path to that is the Message (الرسالة); thus, it was necessary to send a Messenger on the basis of warning (الإنذار) and giving glad tidings (التبشير) regarding the punishment and reward of the Hereafter, and His wrath and pleasure.

The Exalted directed the discourse with a warning by referring to the account of those who disbelieved before, and that they tasted the evil consequences of their conduct, and for them in the Hereafter is a painful punishment. Then He moved to clarify the cause of their disbelief, which was the denial of the Message, and then to the cause of that, which was the denial of the Resurrection and the Return.

Then He concluded from all of that the necessity of their faith in Allah, His Messenger, and the religion He revealed to him. He ended the aforementioned preamble with glad tidings and warning by referring to what has been prepared for the righteous believers of an eternal Paradise, and for others among the denying disbelievers of an everlasting Fire.

So His saying: «أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَبَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ» (**Has there not come to you the account of those who were faithless aforetime?**) is a discourse addressed to the polytheists, containing a reference to the stories of the past perished nations, such as the people of Noah, ‘Ad, Thamud, and others whom Allah destroyed because of their sins. His saying: «فَذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ» (**They tasted the evil consequences of their conduct**) refers to the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال) that descended upon them, and His saying: «وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ» (**and there is a painful punishment for them**) refers to their punishment in the Hereafter.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُ كانَتْ تَأْتِيهِمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَقالُوا أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنا» etc. **That was because their apostles used to come to them with manifest proofs, but they said, ‘Will humans guide us?’** (64:6). This is a clarification of the cause for the mentioned punishment of extirpation and the punishment of the Hereafter. For this reason, the sentence is disconnected (الفصل) rather than joined by a conjunction (العطف), as if it were an answer to a hypothetical question: why did the punishment that struck them strike them? It was said: «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُ كانَتْ» (**That was because...**) etc., and the word "that" (ذلك) refers to the mentioned punishment.

In expressing the arrival of the Messengers and their call with the phrase «كانَتْ تَأْتِيهِمْ» (**used to come to them**), which indicates continuity (الاستمرار), and expressing their disbelief and their statement with the phrases «فَقالُوا» (**but they said**), «فَكَفَرُوا» (**so they disbelieved**), and «تَوَلَّوْا» (**and turned away**), which indicate by contrast a single occurrence, there is an indication that they said what they said as a single, decisive word from which there was no turning back, and they remained firm upon it—which is stubbornness (العناد) and obstinacy (اللجاج). Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to the saying of the Exalted: «تِلْكَ الْقُرى نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ مِنْ أَنْبائِها وَلَقَدْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ كَذلِكَ يَطْبَعُ اللهُ عَلى قُلُوبِ الْكافِرِينَ» **Those are the towns some of whose accounts We relate to you. Their apostles certainly brought them manifest proofs, but they would not believe what they had denied aforetime. Thus does Allah seal the hearts of the faithless.** (7:101), and His saying: «ثُمَّ بَعَثْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِ رُسُلاً إِلى قَوْمِهِمْ فَجاؤُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّناتِ فَما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا بِما كَذَّبُوا بِهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ كَذلِكَ نَطْبَعُ عَلى قُلُوبِ الْمُعْتَدِينَ» **Then, after him, We sent apostles to their people. They brought them manifest proofs, but they would not believe what they had denied aforetime. Thus do We seal the hearts of the transgressors.** (10:74).

His saying: «فَقالُوا أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنا» (**but they said, ‘Will humans guide us?’**): the word "human" (بشر) can be applied to the singular and the plural, and what is meant here is the latter, as evidenced by His saying: «يَهْدُونَنا» (**guide us**). The use of the indefinite (التنكير) is for disparagement (التحقير), and the interrogative is for denial (الإنكار); meaning, they said by way of denial: "Will mere individual humans who have no merit over us guide us?"

This statement of theirs is based on arrogance (الاستكبار), given that most of these perished nations were idolaters (وثنيين) who denied prophethood (النبوة), which is the basis of their denial of the call of the prophets. Therefore, the Exalted derived from their saying «أَبَشَرٌ يَهْدُونَنا» His saying: «فَكَفَرُوا وَتَوَلَّوْا» (**so they disbelieved and turned away**); that is, they built their disbelief and turning away upon that premise.

His saying: «وَاسْتَغْنَى اللهُ» (**and Allah was self-sufficient**): "seeking self-sufficiency" (الاستغناء) when attributed to Allah, the Glorified—who is essentially self-sufficient (غني بالذات)—means the manifestation of self-sufficiency (إظهار الغنى). This is because they used to see themselves as possessing such knowledge, power, and capability as would ward off annihilation from their collective and guarantee them survival, as if existence had no independence from them. This is as Allah, the Glorified, narrated from one of them: «قالَ ما أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هذِهِ أَبَداً» **He said, ‘I do not think this will ever perish’** (18:35), and He said: «وَلئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً» **And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after some adversity that has visited him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due! I do not think the Hour will ever set in.’** (41:50).

The reality of this assumption leads to the idea that Allah, the Glorified, has a need for them and in them—whereas He is essentially self-sufficient. Thus, His destroying and annihilating them is a manifestation from Him of His self-sufficiency from their existence. Accordingly, what is meant by His saying: «وَاسْتَغْنَى اللهُ» is their extirpation, which is indicated by His saying: «فَذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ» (**They tasted the evil consequences of their conduct**).

Furthermore, man is naturally conceited (معجب بنفسه), seeing himself as having a dignity before Allah such that it is necessary for Him to be good to him wherever he may be, as if Allah, the Glorified, had a need to grant him felicity and do good to him, as indicated by the saying of the Exalted: «وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى» **I do not think the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best [reward] with Him.** (41:50), and His saying: «وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلى رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْراً مِنْها مُنْقَلَباً» **I do not think the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find a resort better than this.** (18:36).

The reality of this claim leads to the idea that it is necessary for Allah, the Glorified, to grant them felicity regardless of the circumstances, as if He had a need for them. Thus, His making them taste the evil consequences of their conduct and punishing them in the Hereafter is a manifestation from Him, the Exalted, of His self-sufficiency from them. Therefore, what is meant by His being self-sufficient from them is the sum of what was conveyed by His saying: «فَذاقُوا وَبالَ أَمْرِهِمْ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ» (**They tasted the evil consequences of their conduct, and there is a painful punishment for them**).

These are two views regarding the meaning of the saying of the Exalted: «وَاسْتَغْنَى اللهُ», and the second of them is more comprehensive. In any case, the word possesses a radiance of majesty and power that is not hidden, and it is similar in meaning to His saying: «ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلَنا تَتْرا كُلَّ ما جاءَ أُمَّةً رَسُولُها كَذَّبُوهُ فَأَتْبَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ بَعْضاً وَجعلناهُمْ أَحادِيثَ فَبُعْداً لِقَوْمٍ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ» **Then We sent Our apostles successively. Whenever there came to a nation its apostle, they would impugn him. So We made them follow one another [to extinction] and We made them [the subject of] stories. So away with the people who do not believe!** (23:44).

It is said: what is meant is that Allah was self-sufficient by establishing the proof and completing the argument (إتمام الحجة) against them, needing nothing more than that to guide them to faith.

It is also said: what is meant is that Allah was self-sufficient from their obedience and worship eternally and forever, because He is essentially self-sufficient. Both views are as you see.

His saying: «وَاللهُ غَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ» (**and Allah is all-sufficient, all-laudable**) is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the content of the verse. The meaning is: Allah is self-sufficient in His essence, praiseworthy in what He has done. Thus, what He did to them by making them taste the evil consequences of their conduct and punishing them with a painful punishment for their disbelief and turning away is out of His self-sufficiency and His justice, because it is the requirement of their own deeds returned to them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «زَعَمَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنْ لَنْ يُبْعَثُوا قُلْ بَلى وَرَبِّي لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِما عَمِلْتُمْ وَذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ» **The faithless claim that they will never be resurrected. Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will surely be informed of what you did, and that is easy for Allah.’** (64:7). This mentions another pillar among the pillars of the disbelief of the idolaters, which is their denial of the heavenly religion by denying the Return. For with the negation of the Return, no trace remains of the religion based on command, prohibition, reckoning, and recompense. It also serves as a justification for the denial of the Message, as there would then be no meaning for the conveyance (التبليغ) and the threat (الوعيد).

What is meant by "those who are faithless" (الذين كفروا) is the idolaters in general, including those among them who were contemporaries of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), such as the people of Mecca and its surroundings. It is also said: what is meant is the people of Mecca specifically.

His saying: «قُلْ بَلى وَرَبِّي لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ بِما عَمِلْتُمْ» (**Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will surely be informed of what you did’**) is a command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to respond to their claim that they will never be resurrected, by affirming what they negated with various types of emphasis: the oath (القسم), the *lam* (اللام), and the *nun* (النون).

The word "then" (ثم) in «ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ» indicates a sequence (التراخي) according to the rank of the discourse. The sentence contains a reference to the ultimate purpose of the Resurrection, which is the reckoning (الحساب). His saying: «وَذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ» (**and that is easy for Allah**) means that the mentioned Resurrection and the informing of deeds is easy for Him, the Exalted, and not difficult. In this is a refutation of their deeming the matter of Resurrection impossible for Allah out of perceived improbability. He has expressed this in another place in His speech with the like of His saying: «وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَهُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ» **It is He who originates the creation, and then He brings it back—and that is easier for Him.** (30:27).

The proof for this is what He enumerated at the beginning of the verses of His names and attributes: creation, sovereignty, and knowledge, and that He is glorified and praiseworthy. All of these are gathered in the fact that He is Allah, the one who possesses all attributes of perfection.

It appears from here that the explicit mention of the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة) in the sentence—namely His saying: «وَذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ»—is to hint at the cause (التعليل). The meaning is that this is easy for Him, the Exalted, because He is Allah. Thus, the speech is a demonstrative argument (حجة برهانية) and not a mere claim.

They have mentioned that this verse is the third of the verses in which Allah commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to swear by his Lord regarding the occurrence of the Return. They are three: the first is His saying: «وَيَسْتَنْبِئُونَكَ أَحَقٌّ هُوَ قُلْ إِي وَرَبِّي» **They ask you, ‘Is it true?’ Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord!’** (10:53); the second is His saying: «وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَأْتِينَا السَّاعَةُ قُلْ بَلى وَرَبِّي لَتأْتِيَنَّكُمْ» **The faithless say, ‘The Hour will never overtake us.’ Say, ‘Yes, by my Lord, it will surely overtake you’** (34:3); and the third is the verse we are currently discussing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَآمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَالنُّورِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلْنا وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ» **So have faith in Allah and His Apostle and the Light which We have sent down, and Allah is well aware of what you do.** (64:8). This is a derivation (تفريع) from the content of the previous verse; meaning, if you are inevitably to be resurrected and informed of what you did, then it is incumbent upon you to believe in Allah, His Messenger, and the Light He revealed to His Messenger—which is the Qur’an that guides by its radiant light to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) and clarifies the laws of the religion.

In His saying: «وَالنُّورِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلْنا» (**and the Light which We have sent down**), there is a shift (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person plural (التكلم مع الغير). Perhaps the subtle point in this is the completion of the argument by way of testimony (الشهادة), which is more decisive in cutting off excuses. For how great is the difference between saying: "and the Light which was revealed," which is a report (إخبار), and His saying: «وَالنُّورِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلْنا», for in it is a testimony from Him, the Exalted, that the Qur’an is a heavenly book revealed from Him. Testimony is more emphatic than a mere report.

It should not be said: "What benefit does that have when they deny that the Qur’an is His speech revealed from Him? If they believed that, the previous argument for the Return would have sufficed them and made the shift mentioned unnecessary."

Because it is said: the verses of challenge (آيات التحدي) in the Qur’an, which prove it to be the speech of Allah, are sufficient to invalidate their denial of it being His speech. Testimony, in any case, is more emphatic and stronger than a report, even if the latter is supported by evidence.

His saying: «وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ» (**and Allah is well aware of what you do**) is a reminder of His knowledge of the subtleties of their deeds, so that the command in His saying «فَآمِنُوا» (**So have faith**) may be emphasized by it. The meaning is: believe and be diligent in your faith, for He is knowing of the subtleties of your deeds, not heedless of any of them, and He will inevitably recompense you for them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُكُمْ لِيَوْمِ الْجَمْعِ ذلِكَ يَوْمُ التَّغابُنِ» etc. **The day He will gather you for the Day of Gathering, that will be the day of mutual regret.** (64:9). "The day" (يوم) is an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) for His previous saying: «لَتُبْعَثُنَّ ثُمَّ لَتُنَبَّؤُنَّ» etc. What is meant by the "Day of Gathering" (يوم الجمع) is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) in which people are gathered for the decisive judgment between them. The Exalted said: «وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَجَمَعْناهُمْ جَمْعاً» **and the Trumpet will be blown, and We shall gather them all together** (18:99). The mention of the gathering for the Day of Resurrection has been repeated in the Noble Qur’an, and it is explained by such verses as the saying of the Exalted: «إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ» **Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ** (45:17), and His saying: «فَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ» **Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ** (2:113), and His saying: «إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ» **Indeed your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ** (32:25). Thus, the verses indicate that their gathering is for the purpose of judgment between them.

His saying: «ذلِكَ يَوْمُ التَّغابُنِ» (**that will be the day of mutual regret**): al-Raghib said: "Loss" (الغبن) is to shortchange your counterpart in a transaction between you and him through a kind of concealment. He said: "The Day of Mutual Regret (يوم التغابن) is the Day of Resurrection because of the manifestation of loss in the transaction referred to by His saying: «وَمِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْرِي نَفْسَهُ ابْتِغاءَ مَرْضاتِ اللهِ» **And among the people is he who sells his soul seeking the pleasure of Allah** (2:207), and by His saying: «إِنَّ اللهَ اشْتَرى مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» **Indeed Allah has bought from the faithful...** (9:111), and by His saying: «الَّذِينَ يَشْتَرُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللهِ وَأَيْمانِهِمْ ثَمَناً قَلِيلاً» **those who sell the covenant of Allah and their oaths for a paltry price** (3:77). Thus, they realized that they were shortchanged (غبنوا) in what they abandoned of the transaction and in what they engaged in of it altogether."

Someone was asked about the Day of Mutual Regret and said: "Things will appear to them contrary to their estimations in the world." Here ends the relevant portion.

What he mentioned first is based on interpreting "mutual regret" (التغابن) as the permeation of being shortchanged among the disbelievers by their taking a losing transaction and abandoning a winning one. This is a good meaning, but it does not suit the meaning of the grammatical form *tafa'ul* (تفاعل), which is apparent in the action of some upon others.

What he narrated from someone else is a second view that is not without subtlety. It is supported by such verses as the saying of the Exalted: «فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ» **No one knows what has been kept hidden for them of the delight of the eyes** (32:17), and His saying: «لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ» **There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more** (50:35), and His saying: «وَبَدا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ ما لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ» **and there will appear to them from Allah what they had never expected** (39:47).

The requirement of this view is the universality of mutual regret for all the people of the gathering, both believer and disbeliever. As for the believer, it is because he did not work for his Hereafter more than he did; as for the disbeliever, it is because he did not work at all. The common factor between them is that they did not estimate the Day with its true estimation.

However, the same objection that applies to the previous view applies to this one as well.

There is a third view, which is to consider the mutual regret as occurring between the people of misguidance—their leaders and their followers. The leaders, who are the arrogant ones, shortchange their followers, who are the weak, by commanding them to take the world and abandon the Hereafter, so they go astray. The followers shortchange the leaders by assisting them in their arrogance through their following, so they go astray. Thus, each of the two groups is a shortchanger of the other and shortchanged by the other.

There is a fourth view mentioned in the narrations: that for every servant there is a dwelling in Paradise which he would have entered had he obeyed Allah, and a dwelling in the Fire which he would have entered had he disobeyed Allah. On the Day of Resurrection, the dwellings of the people of the Fire in Paradise are given to the people of Paradise, and the dwellings of the people of Paradise in the Fire are given to the people of the Fire. Thus, the people of Paradise—who are the believers—are the shortchangers of the people of the Fire—who are the disbelievers—and the disbelievers are the ones shortchanged.

One of the commentators said after citing this view: "The mutual regret has been explained by His saying in the following part: «وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ»—until His saying—«وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ»." However, this is not so clearly apparent.

His saying: «وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ وَيَعْمَلْ صالِحاً»—until His saying—«وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ»: its interpretation has preceded repeatedly.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Sahih al-Bukhari*, narrated from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who said: **ما من عبد يدخل الجنة إلا أري مقعده من النار ـ لو أساء ليزداد شكرا. وما من عبد يدخل النار إلا أري مقعده من الجنة ـ لو أحسن ليزداد حسرة.** — "There is no servant who enters Paradise except that he is shown his seat in the Fire—had he done evil—so that he may increase in gratitude. And there is no servant who enters the Fire except that he is shown his seat in Paradise—had he done good—so that he may increase in regret."

**I say:** Regarding this meaning, there are many narrations from both the Sunni and Shi'a paths, and some of them have preceded in the interpretation of the beginning of *Surah al-Mu'minun*.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Babawayh with his chain of narration from Hafs ibn Ghiyath from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: **يوم التلاق يوم يلتقي أهل السماء والأرض ، ويوم التناد يوم ينادي أهل النار أهل الجنة «أَفِيضُوا عَلَيْنا مِنَ الْماءِ أَوْ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ» ويوم التغابن يوم يغبن أهل الجنة أهل النار ، ويوم الحسرة يوم يؤتى بالموت فيذبح.** — "The Day of Encounter (يوم التلاق) is the day the people of heaven and earth encounter one another. The Day of Calling (يوم التناد) is the day the people of the Fire call out to the people of Paradise: ‘Pour some water upon us, or some of what Allah has provided you.’ The Day of Mutual Regret (يوم التغابن) is the day the people of Paradise shortchange the people of the Fire. And the Day of Regret (يوم الحسرة) is the day death is brought and slaughtered."

**I say:** Regarding the concluding verses of the beginning of the *surah* under discussion, there are several narrations that direct the verses toward the affairs of the Guardianship (الولاية), such as what has been narrated that faith and disbelief are faith and disbelief in the Guardianship on the Day of the Covenant (يوم أخذ الميثاق), and what has been narrated that the "manifest proofs" (البينات) are the Imams, and what has been narrated that the "Light" (النور) is the Imam. All of these are looking at the inner depth (بطن) of the verses and are not meant as literal interpretations (مفسرة) at all.
